originalTitle: Senegal bans ministers from foreign travel as oil price rise bites
title: Senegal bans ministers from foreign travel as oil price rise bites
content: The fuel cost is nearly double what the government budgeted for putting pressure on stretched finances.

originalTitle: 'We want a voice in our land' - the people evicted to build Nigeria's capital
title: A Big Story About Homes and Promises in Nigeria!
content:
Hello from Sunny the Reporter!

Hi everyone! It's your friend, Sunny the reporter, right here in beautiful Africa! Today, I have a special story for you. It's about a very important city in Nigeria, called Abuja, and the people who lived there a long, long time ago. Grab your listening ears, because it's a tale about homes, big plans, and promises.

What is a Capital City?

First, let's learn something super cool! Every country has a very important city. We call it the 'capital city'. It's like the main office for the whole country. In this city, important people, like the president and other leaders, work to make rules and help everyone. Did you know? Nigeria's capital city is Abuja! It's a really big and beautiful city now, with tall buildings and wide roads.

Building a Brand New City!

Many years ago, Nigeria needed a new capital city. The old one was getting too crowded. So, the government, who are the grown-ups who help run the country, looked for a new place. They found a lovely spot in the middle of Nigeria. This place was full of green trees, quiet forests, and small, happy villages. Imagine a place where you could see lots of birds and maybe even a few monkeys! That's what it was like. It was a perfect spot for a new, modern city.

Who Lived There Before?

Well, before the big city of Abuja was built, many families lived there. These were not just a few people; there were many, many families! They had lived on that land for a very, very long time, maybe even for hundreds of years. Their great-grandparents, and their great-great-grandparents, had all lived there too. These families had their homes, their farms where they grew yummy food like yams and maize, and their special places for playing and telling stories. They had their schools, their markets, and their churches or mosques. It was their home, their land, and their whole world.

"Please Move!" - A Big Change

Then came the big news! The government told these families that they needed to move. This is a big word, it's called being 'evicted'. It means they were asked to leave their homes, even if they didn't want to. Why? Because the government wanted to build the new, modern capital city of Abuja right there! It must have been a very strange and a little bit scary day for them. Imagine someone telling you to leave your house and all your toys because they want to build something new where your house is!

The Promises!

But the government also made some very important promises. A promise is when someone says they will definitely do something. The government promised that if the families moved, they would help them. They said they would give them: * New homes in a different place. * New land for their farms, so they could still grow food. * Money to help them start fresh. * And even new schools for their children and doctors' clinics for when they were sick. These promises were very important. They made the families feel a little bit better about leaving their old homes. Did you know? Promises are important because they help us trust each other. When you keep a promise, it shows you care!

What Happened Next? The Sad Part

So, the families packed their things and moved. They trusted the government's promises. They looked forward to their new homes and new farms. But, oh dear, here comes the sad part of our story. Many of these promises were not kept. Some families did not get new homes. Others did not get new land to farm. Some did not get the money they were promised. Their children couldn't go to new schools because there weren't any. It was very hard for them. They had left their old homes and now they didn't have the new things they were promised. They felt lost and worried. They had no good place to live, and no good way to earn money or grow food.

They Want a Voice!

Today, many of these people are still waiting. They feel forgotten. They say, "We want a voice in our land!" What does 'having a voice' mean? It means they want to be heard. They want the government to listen to their story. They want to remind everyone about the promises that were made a long time ago. They want help to get the homes, farms, and support they were told they would get. It's like when you try to tell your parents something important, and you want them to really listen to you. These families want the grown-ups in charge to listen and understand their problems. Did you know? Everyone, even little children, should have a voice. It means your ideas and feelings are important!

Why Is This Important?

This story is important because everyone deserves a safe and happy home. Everyone deserves to be treated fairly. And when a promise is made, it should be kept, especially by people who are supposed to help everyone in the country. These families just want what was promised to them. They want to rebuild their lives and have a good future, just like everyone else. They are asking for fairness and for someone to remember them.

What Can We Learn?

We can learn that it's always important to keep our promises. We also learn that it's important to listen to people, especially when they are sad or need help. These families in Nigeria are still hopeful that one day, their voices will be heard, and their promises will finally be fulfilled. Thank you for listening to this important story, my friends! See you next time with more news from Africa! This is Sunny the reporter, signing off! Remember to always be kind and listen to others!
summary: People who were asked to leave their homes to build Nigeria's capital city, Abuja, say the government did not keep its promises to give them new homes and farms, and they want their voices to be heard.
whyMatters: This story matters because everyone deserves a safe home, and governments should always keep their promises to help people, especially when big changes happen.

originalTitle: Burkina Faso must 'forget' about democracy, military leader says
title: Burkina Faso must 'forget' about democracy, military leader says
content: Capt Ibrahim Traoré, who seized power in 2023, says democratic rule would not work for his country.

originalTitle: Haaland resurgence throws down gauntlet for run-in
title: Haaland resurgence throws down gauntlet for run-in
content: Manchester City have beaten Arsenal and taken apart Liverpool in their past two games and Erling Haaland's return to goalscoring form has laid down the gauntlet for the run-in.

title: Haaland hat-trick sinks lacklustre Liverpool
content: Manchester City's Erling Haaland scores a hat-trick in the 4-0 victory against Liverpool, which sends Pep Guardiola's side to the FA Cup semi-finals for an eighth successive season.

originalTitle: Ex-England defender Trippier to leave Newcastle
title: Ex-England defender Trippier to leave Newcastle
